topics.title: Linked List
topics.story: You're at an airport following a treasure hunt. Each clue doesn't give you the treasure — it tells you where the next clue is. That's a linked list: each node holds data and a pointer to the next node. No random access. No jumping to clue #5 without following 1→2→3→4→5.
topics.analogy: A treasure hunt where each clue leads to the next — no map, just one clue at a time. Adding a new clue is easy (slip it anywhere), but finding clue #50 means walking the whole chain.
topics.memoryAnchor: LINKED = each node is LINKED to the next. The last node points to null — the treasure chest.
topics.firstPrinciples: A linked list is a sequence of nodes where each node contains data and a reference to the next node. Time: O(1) insert at known position, O(n) search. Space: O(n). Unlike arrays, nodes are scattered in memory — no indexing, but no shifting on insert/delete.
topics.bruteForce: For most operations (find, delete by value): scan every node sequentially — O(n). For reverse: collect all values, recreate list. Always valid but never fast.
topics.optimizedApproach: Two-pointer (runner) technique for cycle detection, middle finding, kth-from-end. Sentinel/dummy head node to simplify insert/delete at the start. Reverse in-place with three pointers (prev, curr, next).
topics.whenToUse: When you need O(1) insert/delete at the front or at a known position, and sequential access is acceptable. Used in LRU Cache, adjacency lists, undo history.

examples.title: Reverse Linked List — Three Pointer Technique
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":3,"lineCode":"ListNode prev = null, curr = head;","variables":{"prev":"null","curr":"1→2→3→4→5"},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Initialize: prev=null (will be the new tail's next), curr=head=node(1)."},{"line":5,"lineCode":"ListNode next = curr.next;","variables":{"prev":"null","curr":"1","next":"2→3→4→5"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"Save curr.next=2 before we break the link. Critical: without this we lose the rest of the list."},{"line":6,"lineCode":"curr.next = prev;","variables":{"prev":"null","curr":"1→null","next":"2→3→4→5"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"Flip: node(1).next = null (was 2). The link is reversed! node(1) now points backward."},{"line":7,"lineCode":"prev = curr;","variables":{"prev":"1→null","curr":"1","next":"2→3→4→5"},"phase":"ADVANCE","annotation":"Advance prev to curr. prev now marks the end of the reversed portion."},{"line":8,"lineCode":"curr = next;","variables":{"prev":"1→null","curr":"2→3→4→5"},"phase":"ADVANCE","annotation":"Advance curr to saved next. Now process node(2) in the same way."},{"line":3,"lineCode":"while (curr != null) — after all iterations","variables":{"prev":"5→4→3→2→1→null","curr":"null"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"curr=null: loop ends. prev=5→4→3→2→1→null. Return prev — it's the new head!"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A["null ← prev  curr→ 1→2→3→4→5"] --> B["Save next=2"]
    B --> C["Flip: 1→null"]
    C --> D["null←1  curr→2→3→4→5"]
    D --> E["Save next=3, flip 2→1"]
    E --> F["null←1←2  curr→3→4→5"]
    F --> G["... continue ..."];
    G --> H["null←1←2←3←4←5  curr=null"]
    H --> I["Return prev=5 (new head) ✓"]
    style I fill:#d4edda
examples.pseudocode:
FUNCTION reverseList(head):
  prev = null
  curr = head

  WHILE curr != null:
    next = curr.next    // 1. save forward link
    curr.next = prev    // 2. flip: point backward
    prev = curr         // 3. advance prev
    curr = next         // 4. advance curr

  RETURN prev           // prev is new head
examples.explanation: Three pointers work in concert: prev tracks the reversed portion (growing from left), curr is the current node being processed, next saves the forward link before we destroy it. Each node's .next pointer gets flipped once. After n iterations, prev points to the original tail (new head). O(n) time, O(1) space.
examples.realWorldUse: Browser back/forward navigation (reversing history). Undo/redo stacks. In-place reversal of substrings in memory. Doubly-linked list operations in Linux kernel. The pattern of 'three pointers + pointer flip' appears in: reversal of sublists, palindrome check on linked lists, k-group reversal.

examples.title: Floyd's Cycle Detection — Fast and Slow Pointers
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":3,"lineCode":"ListNode slow = head, fast = head;","variables":{"list":"1→2→3→4→2(cycle)","slow":"1","fast":"1"},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"List: 1→2→3→4→2 (4 connects back to 2). Both pointers start at head."},{"line":5,"lineCode":"slow=slow.next; fast=fast.next.next;","variables":{"slow":"2","fast":"3"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"Step 1: slow moves to 2, fast moves to 3. Fast gains 1 node on slow each step."},{"line":5,"lineCode":"slow=slow.next; fast=fast.next.next;","variables":{"slow":"3","fast":"2"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"Step 2: slow=3, fast wraps to 2 (went 3→4→2 via cycle)."},{"line":5,"lineCode":"slow=slow.next; fast=fast.next.next;","variables":{"slow":"4","fast":"4"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"Step 3: slow=4, fast=4. They meet! Cycle confirmed."},{"line":7,"lineCode":"if (slow == fast) return true;","variables":{"slow":"4","fast":"4"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"Pointers meet → cycle exists. Return true. Meeting proves: distance from start to cycle-start equals distance from meeting point to cycle-start."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A["1"] --> B["2"] --> C["3"] --> D["4"]
    D --> B
    E["slow: 1→2→3→4"] --> F["fast: 1→3→2→4"]
    F --> G["slow==fast at node 4 → CYCLE!"]
    style G fill:#f8d7da
examples.explanation: Floyd's algorithm: fast pointer moves 2x speed of slow. In a cycle of length L, fast gains 1 step on slow per iteration. They must meet within L steps. Proof: once both are in the cycle, the distance between them decreases by 1 each step until it hits 0 (meeting). The bonus: resetting slow to head and moving both 1 step at a time finds the cycle start — mathematical proof: distance(head→start) = distance(meeting→start).
examples.realWorldUse: Detecting infinite loops in linked data structures. Finding the start of circular references in garbage collectors. Detecting cycles in state machines. The same pattern (slow/fast pointers) is used for: finding the middle of a list in one pass, detecting palindromes, kth-from-end in one pass.

examples.title: Find Middle — Runner Technique
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":4,"lineCode":"ListNode slow = head, fast = head;","variables":{"list":"1→2→3→4→5","slow":"1","fast":"1"},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"5-node list. Both start at node 1. Fast moves 2x speed."},{"line":5,"lineCode":"slow=slow.next; fast=fast.next.next;","variables":{"slow":"2","fast":"3"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"Step 1: slow at 2, fast at 3."},{"line":5,"lineCode":"slow=slow.next; fast=fast.next.next;","variables":{"slow":"3","fast":"5"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"Step 2: slow at 3, fast at 5."},{"line":5,"lineCode":"fast=null after fast.next.next","variables":{"slow":"3","fast":"null"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"Step 3: fast.next=null, loop exits. slow=3 is the middle."},{"line":8,"lineCode":"return slow;","variables":{"middle":"3"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"Middle found in ONE PASS. For 4-node list: slow stops at node 2 (first of two middles) or node 3 (second). Depends on starting condition."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A["Step 0: S=1, F=1"] --> B["Step 1: S=2, F=3"]
    B --> C["Step 2: S=3, F=5"]
    C --> D["Step 3: F.next=null, stop"]
    D --> E["Middle = slow = 3 ✓"]
    style E fill:#d4edda
    F["1→2→3→4→5"] --> G["S moves 1 step"]
    F --> H["F moves 2 steps"]
examples.explanation: The runner technique exploits the 2x speed ratio: when fast completes the journey (reaches null), slow has traveled exactly half the distance = the middle. One pass, no counting, no second pass. For even-length lists, slow lands on the second middle (useful for merge sort: cut at mid.next = null). Used inside merge sort on linked lists for O(n log n) sort with O(log n) stack space.
examples.realWorldUse: Merge sort on linked lists (LeetCode 148). Palindrome check: find middle, reverse second half, compare. Splitting a list into two halves for parallel processing. The pattern generalizes: to find the kth-from-end node, start fast k steps ahead of slow, then move both until fast=null.

examples.title: Merge Two Sorted Lists — Dummy Head Pattern
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":3,"lineCode":"ListNode dummy = new ListNode(0);","variables":{"l1":"1→3→5","l2":"2→4→6","dummy":"0","tail":"0"},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Dummy head: avoids special-casing the first node. tail always points to the last node in the merged list."},{"line":7,"lineCode":"if (l1.val <= l2.val)","variables":{"l1.val":1,"l2.val":2,"tail.next":"1","l1":"3→5"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"1 ≤ 2: take from l1. tail.next=node(1), advance l1 to 3, advance tail to 1."},{"line":10,"lineCode":"} else {","variables":{"l1.val":3,"l2.val":2,"tail.next":"2","l2":"4→6"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"3 > 2: take from l2. tail.next=node(2), advance l2 to 4, advance tail to 2."},{"line":7,"lineCode":"if (l1.val <= l2.val)","variables":{"l1.val":3,"l2.val":4,"tail.next":"3"},"phase":"PROCESS","annotation":"3 ≤ 4: take 3 from l1. Continue..."},{"line":16,"lineCode":"tail.next = (l1 != null) ? l1 : l2;","variables":{"remaining":"l2=6"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"l1 exhausted first. Attach remaining l2 (node 6). merged: 1→2→3→4→5→6."},{"line":17,"lineCode":"return dummy.next;","variables":{"result":"1→2→3→4→5→6"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"Return dummy.next skipping the sentinel 0. Merged list is complete. O(n+m) time, O(1) space."}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A["dummy→null\nl1:1→3→5  l2:2→4→6"] --> B["1<2: take l1\ndummy→1"]
    B --> C["3>2: take l2\ndummy→1→2"]
    C --> D["3<4: take l1\ndummy→1→2→3"]
    D --> E["5>4: take l2\ndummy→1→2→3→4"]
    E --> F["5<6: take l1\ndummy→1→2→3→4→5"]
    F --> G["l1=null: attach l2=6"]
    G --> H["Result: 1→2→3→4→5→6 ✓"]
    style H fill:#d4edda
examples.pseudocode:
FUNCTION mergeTwoLists(l1, l2):
  dummy = new Node(0)   // sentinel — avoids edge cases
  tail = dummy

  WHILE l1 != null AND l2 != null:
    IF l1.val <= l2.val:
      tail.next = l1; l1 = l1.next
    ELSE:
      tail.next = l2; l2 = l2.next
    tail = tail.next

  // One list exhausted — attach the rest
  tail.next = l1 if l1!=null else l2
  RETURN dummy.next    // skip sentinel
examples.explanation: The dummy head pattern eliminates the special case of inserting the first node. Without it, you'd need 'if result == null: result = node, else: tail.next = node'. With dummy, every append is identical. Two pointers on two sorted lists: always pick the smaller head. When one list exhausts, attach the other's remaining tail (already sorted). O(n+m) time, O(1) space — no new nodes created.
examples.realWorldUse: Merge K sorted lists (heap or divide-and-conquer over pairs). Merge step in linked list merge sort. Merging sorted streams in real-time data pipelines. The dummy-head pattern appears whenever you build a linked list incrementally and want clean, uniform append logic.

examples.title: Remove Nth Node from End — One Pass with Gap
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":4,"lineCode":"ListNode dummy = new ListNode(0); dummy.next = head;","variables":{"list":"0→1→2→3→4→5","n":2},"phase":"INIT","annotation":"Dummy head prepended. Remove 2nd from end (node 4 in 1→2→3→4→5). fast=slow=dummy."},{"line":8,"lineCode":"for (int i = 0; i <= n; i++) fast = fast.next;","variables":{"fast":"3","slow":"0(dummy)"},"phase":"GAP","annotation":"Advance fast n+1=3 steps from dummy: 0→1→2→3. Gap between fast(3) and slow(0) is 3 nodes."},{"line":11,"lineCode":"while (fast != null)","variables":{"fast":"4","slow":"1"},"phase":"MOVE","annotation":"Step 1: fast=4, slow=1. Gap maintained at 3."},{"line":11,"lineCode":"while (fast != null)","variables":{"fast":"5","slow":"2"},"phase":"MOVE","annotation":"Step 2: fast=5, slow=2."},{"line":11,"lineCode":"while (fast != null)","variables":{"fast":"null","slow":"3"},"phase":"MOVE","annotation":"Step 3: fast=null, loop ends. slow=3 is the node BEFORE the target (node 4)."},{"line":16,"lineCode":"slow.next = slow.next.next;","variables":{"removed":"node(4)","list":"1→2→3→5"},"phase":"COMPLETE","annotation":"Skip node 4: slow.next=node(5). Return dummy.next=1→2→3→5. Done in ONE pass!"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A["dummy→1→2→3→4→5  n=2"] --> B["Advance fast 3 steps"]
    B --> C["slow=dummy, fast=3"]
    C --> D["Move both until fast=null"]
    D --> E["fast=null, slow=node(3)"]
    E --> F["slow.next = slow.next.next"]
    F --> G["1→2→3→5 (4 removed) ✓"]
    style G fill:#d4edda
examples.pseudocode:
FUNCTION removeNthFromEnd(head, n):
  dummy = Node(0); dummy.next = head
  fast = slow = dummy

  // Create gap of n+1 between fast and slow
  FOR i = 0 to n: fast = fast.next

  // Move in lockstep until fast falls off
  WHILE fast != null:
    slow = slow.next
    fast = fast.next

  // slow is just before target node
  slow.next = slow.next.next
  RETURN dummy.next
examples.explanation: The gap technique: advance fast n+1 steps ahead of slow. When fast reaches null, slow is exactly n+1 nodes from the end, meaning slow.next is the nth-from-end node. The dummy head handles the edge case where the head itself needs removal (n = list length). One pass O(n), O(1) space — no need to compute list length first.
examples.realWorldUse: Any 'kth from end' problem. Sliding window on linked structures. The gap technique is the linked list analog of binary search on arrays — it avoids two passes (count + traverse) by maintaining a fixed-distance relationship between two pointers.

problems.title: Reverse a Linked List
problems.description:
Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse the list and return the reversed list.

**Example:**
Input: 1→2→3→4→5
Output: 5→4→3→2→1

**Example 2:**
Input: 1→2
Output: 2→1
problems.hint1: Use three pointers: prev, curr, and next. What does 'next' need to save before you break curr.next?
problems.hint2: In each step: (1) save curr.next in a temp variable, (2) point curr.next to prev, (3) advance prev to curr, (4) advance curr to saved next.
problems.hint3: Initialize prev = null, curr = head. Loop while curr != null. Return prev at the end — it's the new head.
problems.editorial: Iterative: maintain prev=null, curr=head. In each step save curr.next, flip curr.next=prev, advance both. O(n) time, O(1) space. Recursive: reverse(head.next), then head.next.next=head, head.next=null. O(n) space from call stack.

problems.title: Detect Cycle in Linked List
problems.description:
Given the head of a linked list, determine if the linked list has a cycle. A cycle exists if there is some node in the list that can be reached again by continuously following the next pointer.

**Example:**
Input: head = [3,2,0,-4], pos = 1 (tail connects to node at index 1)
Output: true

**Example 2:**
Input: head = [1,2], pos = -1 (no cycle)
Output: false
problems.hint1: Can you use two pointers moving at different speeds? What happens if one moves twice as fast?
problems.hint2: Floyd's Cycle Detection: slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 steps. If there's a cycle, fast will 'lap' slow and they'll meet.
problems.hint3: If fast == null or fast.next == null, there's no cycle (fast reached the end). If slow == fast, a cycle exists.
problems.editorial: Floyd's Cycle Detection: O(n) time, O(1) space. slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2. If cycle exists, fast laps slow and they meet inside the cycle. Alternative: HashSet of visited nodes — O(n) space.

problems.title: Find Middle of Linked List
problems.description:
Given the head of a singly linked list, return the middle node. If there are two middle nodes, return the second middle node.

**Example:**
Input: [1,2,3,4,5] → Output: node 3
Input: [1,2,3,4,5,6] → Output: node 4 (second middle)
problems.hint1: Can you reach the middle in one pass without knowing the length?
problems.hint2: Use two pointers. When the fast pointer reaches the end, where is the slow pointer?
problems.hint3: slow=head, fast=head. Move slow 1 step, fast 2 steps. When fast==null or fast.next==null, slow is at the middle.
problems.editorial: Runner technique: fast at 2x speed. When fast hits the end, slow is at floor(n/2)+1 — which is exactly the second middle for even-length lists. O(n) time, O(1) space.

problems.title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
problems.description:
Merge two sorted linked lists and return it as a sorted list.

**Example:**
Input: l1 = [1,2,4], l2 = [1,3,4]
Output: [1,1,2,3,4,4]
problems.hint1: Use a dummy head node to avoid special-casing the head of the merged list.
problems.hint2: Compare l1.val and l2.val. Attach the smaller one to your result, and advance that pointer.
problems.hint3: After the loop (one list exhausted), attach the remaining nodes of the other list — no need to iterate, just point tail.next = remaining.
problems.constraints: 0 ≤ length of each list ≤ 50 | -100 ≤ Node.val ≤ 100 | Both lists are sorted in non-decreasing order
problems.editorial: Dummy head simplifies code — no special case for empty result. Iteratively pick smaller head. After loop, at most one list remains — attach entire tail. O(m+n) time, O(1) space.

problems.title: Remove Nth Node From End
problems.description:
Given the head of a linked list, remove the nth node from the end of the list and return its head.

**Example:**
Input: head = [1,2,3,4,5], n = 2
Output: [1,2,3,5]  (removed 4, which is 2nd from end)
problems.hint1: Can you find the nth from end in one pass? How would two pointers with a gap of n help?
problems.hint2: Advance the fast pointer n+1 steps first (using a dummy head). Then move both slow and fast together until fast is null. Now slow.next is the node to remove.
problems.hint3: Use a dummy node before head so you can delete the head node without special-casing. slow starts at dummy, fast starts at dummy. Advance fast n+1 steps, then move both until fast==null.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ number of nodes ≤ 30 | 0 ≤ Node.val ≤ 100 | 1 ≤ n ≤ number of nodes
problems.editorial: Two-pointer with n+1 gap (advance fast n+1 steps, not n, because we need slow to stop at the node BEFORE the one to delete). Dummy node handles head deletion cleanly. O(n) time, O(1) space.

problems.title: Check if Linked List is Palindrome
problems.description:
Given the head of a singly linked list, return true if it is a palindrome.

**Example:**
Input: [1,2,2,1] → Output: true
Input: [1,2] → Output: false
problems.hint1: How can you compare the first half with the second half without using extra space?
problems.hint2: Find the middle, reverse the second half in-place, then compare both halves node by node.
problems.hint3: Steps: (1) find middle using fast/slow, (2) reverse from mid to end, (3) compare head and reversed-half simultaneously, (4) optionally restore the list.
problems.editorial: Three phases: find middle O(n), reverse second half O(n), compare O(n). Total O(n) time, O(1) space. The in-place reversal is the key — avoid the O(n) array approach.

problems.title: Add Two Numbers as Linked Lists
problems.description:
Two non-empty linked lists represent two non-negative integers in reverse order (ones digit first). Add the numbers and return the sum as a linked list.

**Example:**
Input: l1 = [2,4,3], l2 = [5,6,4]
Output: [7,0,8]  (342 + 465 = 807)
problems.hint1: Simulate grade-school addition: add corresponding digits plus carry. What do you output if both lists are exhausted but carry is 1?
problems.hint2: Use a dummy head. Loop while l1 != null OR l2 != null OR carry != 0. Take l1.val (0 if null), l2.val (0 if null), add them + carry.
problems.hint3: sum = (l1?.val ?? 0) + (l2?.val ?? 0) + carry. New node value = sum % 10. carry = sum / 10. Advance l1 and l2 if not null.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ number of nodes ≤ 100 | 0 ≤ Node.val ≤ 9 | No leading zeros except the number 0 itself
problems.editorial: Simulate addition column by column. The `|| carry != 0` in the loop condition handles the final carry digit (e.g., 999 + 1 = 1000). O(max(m,n)) time and space.

problems.title: LRU Cache
problems.description:
Design a data structure that follows the LRU (Least Recently Used) cache eviction policy.

Implement LRUCache(int capacity), int get(int key), void put(int key, int value).
- get: Return value if key exists, else -1. Mark as recently used.
- put: Insert or update. If capacity exceeded, evict the LRU item.

Both operations must be O(1).
problems.hint1: You need O(1) lookup AND O(1) delete-from-any-position AND O(1) insert-at-front. Which data structures give you each?
problems.hint2: HashMap gives O(1) lookup by key. Doubly linked list gives O(1) insert/delete at any position (if you have the node reference). Combine them.
problems.hint3: HashMap maps key → DLL node. The DLL is ordered by recency (head=most recent, tail=LRU). On get/put: move node to head. On eviction: remove tail. Use dummy head and tail sentinels.
problems.constraints: 1 ≤ capacity ≤ 3000 | 0 ≤ key ≤ 10^4 | 0 ≤ value ≤ 10^5 | At most 2*10^5 calls to get and put
problems.editorial: HashMap + Doubly Linked List. HashMap gives O(1) key lookup. DLL gives O(1) move-to-front and O(1) remove-from-tail. Dummy sentinel head/tail nodes eliminate null checks. Classic system design + DSA hybrid problem.

problems.title: Copy List with Random Pointer
problems.description:
A linked list is given where each node has a `next` pointer and a `random` pointer (can point to any node or null). Return a deep copy of the list.

**Example:**
Input: [[7,null],[13,0],[11,4],[10,2],[1,0]]
(each pair is [val, random_index])
Output: Deep copy with same structure
problems.hint1: The challenge is random pointers — you can't set them until all nodes are created. How do you map old nodes to new nodes?
problems.hint2: Use a HashMap: old_node → new_node. First pass: create all new nodes and populate the map. Second pass: set next and random pointers using the map.
problems.hint3: For each old node: newNode.next = map.get(old.next), newNode.random = map.get(old.random). Both can be null — map.get(null) returns null safely.
problems.constraints: 0 ≤ n ≤ 1000 | -10^4 ≤ Node.val ≤ 10^4 | Node.random is null or points to some node in the list
problems.editorial: Two-pass HashMap: O(n) time, O(n) space. Pass 1 creates all clone nodes. Pass 2 wires next and random using the map. O(1) space alternative: interleave clones between original nodes (weave technique), set randoms, then unweave.

problems.title: Flatten a Multilevel Doubly Linked List
problems.description:
A doubly linked list may have nodes with a `child` pointer to another doubly linked list. Flatten the list so all nodes appear in a single-level list. The child list should be inserted between the node and its next.

**Example:**
1 - 2 - 3 - 4 - 5
        |child
        7 - 8 - 9
Output: 1 - 2 - 3 - 7 - 8 - 9 - 4 - 5
problems.hint1: When you find a node with a child, you need to splice the child list between the current node and its next. What information do you need to save before unlinking?
problems.hint2: Save curr.next (the node after curr). Connect curr → child. Connect end of child list → saved next. Clear curr.child.
problems.hint3: Use a stack or recursion: when you encounter a child, push next to a stack and follow the child. When you hit null, pop from the stack to continue.
problems.editorial: Iterative in-place: when child found, find child's tail O(child length), splice. Total O(n) since each node visited at most twice. Stack-based approach: O(n) time, O(depth) space — push next before following child.
